texts.label: text
texts.orig: ABSTRACT. We provide explicit descriptions for the rational powers and Rees valuations of several classes of ideals invariant under natural actions of tori and products of general linear groups, in terms of polyhedra and lattice points. This allows us to show that a version of Musta¸ t˘ a-Takagi's summation formula for multiplier ideals also holds for the rational powers of these ideals. Moreover, for arbitrary ideals in normal domains that are finitely generated over algebraically closed fields, we prove a weaker version of this formula that holds for sufficiently large rational numbers.
texts.text: ABSTRACT. We provide explicit descriptions for the rational powers and Rees valuations of several classes of ideals invariant under natural actions of tori and products of general linear groups, in terms of polyhedra and lattice points. This allows us to show that a version of Musta¸ t˘ a-Takagi's summation formula for multiplier ideals also holds for the rational powers of these ideals. Moreover, for arbitrary ideals in normal domains that are finitely generated over algebraically closed fields, we prove a weaker version of this formula that holds for sufficiently large rational numbers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Rees valuations and rational powers of ideals play a central role in many results in algebraic geometry and commutative algebra [3, 12, 13, 20, 26, 27, 36, 39, 43, 46, 52]. These two sets of invariants are so closely related that each uniquely determines the other (see Remark 2.11). However, explicitly computing them is a difficult task because it requires determining the order valuations for every prime divisor arising in the exceptional divisor of the normalized blowup of the ideal [50]. In fact, complete descriptions of Rees valuations in the literature are limited to monomial ideals, where they correspond to supporting hyperplanes of Newton polyhedra [38, Theorem 10.3.5]. One of the primary goals of this paper is to provide explicit descriptions of both Rees valuations and rational powers for several classes of ideals in polynomial rings that are invariant under natural actions of tori and products of general linear groups.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Originally considered by Samuel and Rees in the 1950s [50,54], the notion of rational powers of an ideal I in a Noetherian domain R was introduced by Lejeune-Jalabert and Teissier [45, §4.2]. The power I w for w ∈ Q ⩾ 0 is defined as I w : = ∩ v { x | v ( x ) ⩾ wv ( I ) } , where the intersection is taken over all the discrete valuations of Quot ( R ) that are nonnegative in R , or alternatively, over the Rees valuations of I . This construction provides a powerful tool for understanding the structure of filtrations of ideals such as integral closures [36,39], symbolic powers [26], Frobenius powers [52], and multiplier ideals [44, §9.6]. Despite its historical roots, rational powers have seen limited development, as noted by Knutson in 2010, who observed that this 'beautiful construction has sat essentially unused in the literature for over 50 years now" [42].

texts.orig: Let X = Xm × n be a generic m × n matrix, meaning its entries are distinct variables. The polynomial ring C [ X ] carries a natural action of GL m ( C ) × GL n ( C ) defined by ( A , B ) · X = A -1 XB , where A ∈ GL m ( C ) and B ∈ GL n ( C ) . The ideals in the polynomial ring C [ X ] that are invariant under this action have been extensively studied in the literature, see e.g. [6,7,10,17,18,21,35,40]. The combination of representation theory and the theory of algebras with straightening laws has led to a wide range of explicit results about the structure and properties of these ideals. If one considers a symmetric generic matrix Y = Yn × n and GL n ( C ) -action on C [ Y ] given by A · Y = AYA t , or a skew-symmetric generic matrix Z = Zn × n with GL n ( C ) -action on C [ Z ] given by A · Z = AZA t , one obtains analogous well-studied invariant ideals, see e.g. the references above and [1,2,14,19].

texts.orig: We introduce the concept of Rees package , which is a combinatorial characteristic-free construction that encapsulates the description of both Rees valuations and rational powers in terms of polyhedral data (see Definition 3.5). In our first theorem, we show that the classes of invariant ideals described above admit such a Rees package. Furthermore, in arbitrary characteristic, we show that monomial ideals in affine semigroup rings, sums of products of determinantal ideals of generic, symmetric, and skew-symmetric matrices, and products of determinantal ideals of Hankel matrices, also admit a Rees package.

texts.orig: In [49], Musta¸ t˘ a showed a summation formula for multiplier ideals of pairs ideal sheaves, and this formula was later generalized by Takagi [56] and by Jow-Miller [41]. Musta¸ t˘ a-Takagi summation formula raises the natural question of whether similar identities hold for symbolic powers and rational powers of ideals. The former was established in [31,32] and the latter in [4] for monomial ideals. Thus we propose the following conjecture (cf. [4, Question 2.4]).

First, we note that the restriction on the field is partially justified by a related question (see Question 4.14 and Example 4.15). texts.orig: In Proposition 4.7 we show that if two ideals I ⊂ R and J ⊂ S admit a Rees package, e.g. the invariant ideals in Theorem A, then IT + JT does as well. Moreover, a Rees package for IT + JT can be obtained by 'gluing' those of I and J . In particular, this shows that the Rees valuations of IT + JT are obtained by pairing the ones of I and J (see Corollary 4.12). This result allows us to compute rational powers of more classes of ideals, see for instance Example 4.10.

texts.orig: In this section we review and extend some relevant facts about rational powers and Rees valuations. Our main references in this subject is [38]. We note that several of the statements in this section have been previously established for the integral closures of powers of ideals. Our goal is to show that these properties continue to hold in the more general setting of rational powers and to provide references for the corresponding assertions. We restrict our exposition to the following setup where all of our results occur.

texts.orig: Proposition 2.4. Adopt Setup 2.1. For every multiplicative subset W ⊂ R and rational number w ∈ Q ⩾ 0 we have W -1 I w =( W -1 I ) w .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. If w is an integer, the statement follows from [38, Proposition 10.5.5]. Now let w be arbitrary and write w = p q . If x ∈ I w , then ( W -1 x ) q = W -1 x q ∈ W -1 I p = ( W -1 I ) p and so W -1 x ∈ ( W -1 I ) w . On the other hand, if y ∈ ( W -1 I ) w , then y q ∈ ( W -1 I ) p = W -1 I p . Therefore there exists a ∈ W such that ay q ∈ I p . The latter implies ( ay ) q ∈ I p and so ay ∈ I w . Thus y ∈ W -1 I w , finishing the proof. □

texts.orig: A valuation is discrete if it is integer valued. Integral closures and rational powers can also be defined via discrete valuations: by [38, Theorem 6.8.3(3)] we have that x ∈ I if and only if x ∈ IV for every discrete valuation ring R ⊆ V ⊆ Quot ( R ) . Moreover, the latter condition can be verified by using finitely many valuations, the Rees valuations of I .

Integral closures and rational powers can also be defined via discrete valuations: by [38, Theorem 6.8.3(3)] we have that x ∈ I if and only if x ∈ IV for every discrete valuation ring R ⊆ V ⊆ Quot ( R ) . texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.5 (Rees valuations) . Adopt Setup 2.1. Let I =( a 1 , . . . , ad ) be a set of generators and, for each i = 1 , . . . , d , set Ri = R [ a 1 ai , . . . , ad ai ] and Ri its integral closure in Quot ( Ri ) = Quot ( R ) . Let RV ( I ) be the set of distinct discrete valuation rings arising as V = ( Ri ) p , where p varies over the minimal primes of IRi = aiRi . The set RV ( I ) is called the Rees valuations rings of I . We will often abuse notation and identify each V ∈ RV ( I ) with any valuation v which is a positive integer multiple of the order valuation of V .
texts.text: Definition 2.5 (Rees valuations) . Adopt Setup 2.1. Let I =( a 1 , . . . , ad ) be a set of generators and, for each i = 1 , . . . , d , set Ri = R [ a 1 ai , . . . , ad ai ] and Ri its integral closure in Quot ( Ri ) = Quot ( R ) . Let RV ( I ) be the set of distinct discrete valuation rings arising as V = ( Ri ) p , where p varies over the minimal primes of IRi = aiRi . The set RV ( I ) is called the Rees valuations rings of I . We will often abuse notation and identify each V ∈ RV ( I ) with any valuation v which is a positive integer multiple of the order valuation of V .

texts.orig: Proof. Fix f = ∑ ci ∈ k ∗ ci b ℓ i and g = ∑ ei ∈ k ∗ ei b µ i in R . Since V is B -monomial, we clearly have V ( f + g ) ⩾ min { V ( f ) , V ( g ) } . Notice that V ( f g ) = V ( f ) + V ( g ) is equivalent to V ( b ) = V ( f ) + V ( g ) for some b ∈ B in the expansion of f g . To show the existence of such b we can further assume that for every i one has V ( b ℓ i ) = V ( f ) and V ( b µ i ) = V ( g ) . Let P f : = conv ( { v ( b ℓ i ) } ) and P g : = conv ( { v ( b µ i ) } ) and notice that these polytopes belong to the parallel hyperplanes ⟨ h , X ⟩ = V ( f ) and ⟨ h , X ⟩ = V ( g ) , respectively. Consider a vertex a of the Minkowski sum P f + P g . Therefore, there exist unique a f ∈ P f ∩ Z d ⩾ 0 and a g ∈ P g ∩ Z d ⩾ 0 such that a f + a g = a , and then we can assume v ( b ℓ i ) = a f and v ( b µ j ) = a g for every i , j . The statement now follows by the assumption. □

texts.orig: 3.2. Sums of products of determinantal ideals and GL m ( C ) × GL n ( C ) -invariant ideals. In this subsection we show that ideals obtained by taking sums of products of determinantal ideals in a generic matrix X have a Rees package. As a consequence we obtain that GL m ( C ) × GL n ( C ) -invariant ideals of the polynomial ring C [ X ] have a Rees package. Throughout we follow the treatment in [6, 8, 17, 35].
texts.text: 3.2. Sums of products of determinantal ideals and GL m ( C ) × GL n ( C ) -invariant ideals. In this subsection we show that ideals obtained by taking sums of products of determinantal ideals in a generic matrix X have a Rees package. As a consequence we obtain that GL m ( C ) × GL n ( C ) -invariant ideals of the polynomial ring C [ X ] have a Rees package. Throughout we follow the treatment in [6, 8, 17, 35].
